title: How sleep deprivation compounds trauma symptoms

htmlContent:
The night stretches out like an open wound

I've been there. Lying in the dark, watching the clock tick past 2 AM, then 3, then 4. Your body is exhausted but your brain won't shut up. Every sound makes you flinch. Every memory that surfaces feels like a fresh cut. You're not just tired. You're raw. Exposed. Like someone peeled back your skin and left all your nerve endings hanging out in the open air.

Here's the thing about trauma and sleep. They have a relationship that's toxic. Codependent. Destructive. And if you're living with complex trauma, you already know this dance. You know that when sleep goes, everything else follows. Your patience. Your ability to regulate. Your grip on reality itself.

But there's actual science behind this. Real research that explains why sleep deprivation doesn't just make you groggy. It actively makes your trauma symptoms worse. Stay with me here.

What happens to the traumatized brain when it doesn't sleep

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote the book on trauma and the body, has spent decades showing us that trauma isn't just a story we tell. It's a physical state. A nervous system that's stuck in survival mode. And sleep? Sleep is when your nervous system is supposed to reset. When your amygdala gets a break. When your prefrontal cortex gets to file away the day's experiences and calm things down.

But when you're not sleeping, none of that happens. Your amygdala stays on high alert. Your cortisol stays elevated. Your body keeps pumping out stress hormones because it thinks you're still in danger. And the thing is, your body isn't wrong. It's just stuck. It's trying to protect you from a threat that's no longer there, but it doesn't know how to turn off the alarm.

I remember a period in my life when I was getting maybe three hours of sleep a night. I was jumpy. Irritable. I'd hear a car door slam outside and my heart would start racing like someone was breaking in. I'd have flashbacks that felt more real than the room I was standing in. And I'd think, "What's wrong with me? Why can't I just get it together?"

But here's what I didn't know then. Sleep deprivation and trauma symptoms share a lot of the same brain chemistry. Hypervigilance. Emotional dysregulation. Difficulty concentrating. Memory problems. When you're not sleeping, you're recreating the same neural environment that trauma creates. You're pouring gasoline on a fire that's already burning.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). Deb Dana, who works directly with Stephen Porges, shows how our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety or threat. And when we're sleep deprived, that scan tilts hard toward threat. Everything feels dangerous. Everything feels like too much.

The body keeps the score, especially when it's exhausted

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets trapped in the body. It's not just in your head. It's in your shoulders, your jaw, your gut, your pelvis. And when you're not sleeping, your body doesn't get the chance to complete those incomplete stress responses. You stay in freeze. Or fight. Or flight. You never get to the rest and digest state that sleep is supposed to provide.

I notice this in my own body right now. When I've had a bad night of sleep, my shoulders are up by my ears. My jaw is clenched. My breathing is shallow. I'm holding tension in places I didn't even know I had muscles. And that tension isn't just uncomfortable. It's sending signals to my brain that say, "We are still in danger. Stay alert. Don't relax."

And then the cycle continues. I'm too tense to sleep. I don't sleep. I get more tense. More reactive. More triggered. More dissociated. It's a feedback loop that feels impossible to break.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium glycinate is known for helping with muscle relaxation and sleep quality. It's not a cure. But for some of us, it takes the edge off enough to let the body start settling. Wild, right? That something as simple as a mineral can help calm a nervous system that's been screaming for years.

But here's the thing. Supplements aren't enough on their own. You can't magnesium your way out of complex trauma. You need to address the underlying patterns. The beliefs. The ways your nervous system learned to stay vigilant because being vigilant kept you alive.

The shame spiral that comes with not sleeping

This is the part that doesn't get talked about enough. The shame. The self-blame. The voice in your head that says, "If you could just get your act together, you'd be able to sleep. What's wrong with you? Other people can sleep. Why can't you?"

That voice is loud. And it's cruel. And it makes everything worse because now you're not just sleep deprived and traumatized. You're also ashamed of being sleep deprived and traumatized. You're beating yourself up for a symptom of your injury.

Think about it. If someone had a broken leg, you wouldn't tell them to just walk it off. You wouldn't shame them for limping. But trauma and sleep, we internalize this idea that we should be able to control it. That if we just tried harder, meditated more, took the right supplements, did the right breathing exercises, we'd be fine.

That's toxic positivity dressed up as self-help. And it's a lie.

Gabor Mate, the physician who studies trauma and addiction, talks about how trauma isn't what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. And what happens inside you when you can't sleep is a whole cascade of physiological and psychological responses that you didn't choose. That you didn't cause. That you don't deserve to be punished for.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link). Oprah and Dr. Bruce Perry ask that question instead of "What's wrong with you?" It shifts the entire framework. And when you apply that same shift to sleep, it changes things. Instead of "Why can't I sleep?" you can ask "What happened to my nervous system that makes sleep feel unsafe?"

Because for a lot of us, sleep does feel unsafe. When you grew up in an environment where being unconscious meant being vulnerable to harm, your body learned that staying awake was survival. Your body learned that sleep was dangerous. And your body is still trying to protect you, even if that protection is now working against you.

What actually helps when you're stuck in the cycle

I'm not going to give you a list of ten easy steps. That would be dishonest. Healing from complex trauma is not linear, and sleep is often one of the last things to stabilize. But there are things that can help. Small things. Gentle things. Things that don't require you to be perfect.

First, stop trying to force sleep. The more you try to control it, the more your nervous system resists. It's like trying to force yourself to relax. It doesn't work. It creates more tension. Instead, focus on what Deb Dana calls "glimmers." Small moments of safety. A warm cup of tea. A soft blanket. The sound of rain. A book that doesn't trigger you.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Gabor Mate shows how chronic stress and unexpressed emotions can literally make us sick. And sleep deprivation is one of the ways the body says no. It's a signal. A message. Not a failure.

Second, create a bedtime routine that feels safe, not rigid. Maybe it's five minutes of gentle stretching. Maybe it's writing down what you're afraid of. Maybe it's putting one hand on your heart and one hand on your belly and just breathing. Not to fall asleep. Just to be present. To let your body know it's okay to be here, in this moment, without having to do anything.

Third, consider working with a therapist who understands trauma and sleep. Someone who knows that you can't just "sleep hygiene" your way out of this. Someone who will sit with you in the hard nights instead of telling you to just try harder.

And fourth, be honest about where you are. Some nights you're going to be awake. Some nights the flashbacks are going to come. Some nights you're going to feel like you're losing your mind. And on those nights, the goal isn't to sleep. The goal is to survive. To get through the night without hurting yourself. To remind yourself that this is temporary. That morning comes. That you have made it through every single bad night so far, and you will make it through this one too.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did your shoulders drop even a little? Did your breath deepen? That's your nervous system responding to the possibility of safety. That's a glimmer. Hold onto it.

The relationship between sleep deprivation and trauma symptoms is real. It's brutal. And it's not your fault. Your body learned to stay awake because staying awake kept you alive. But now you get to teach it, slowly, gently, imperfectly, that it's okay to rest. That you are safe enough. That you will be okay.

It takes time. It takes patience. It takes a lot of nights that feel like failures. But your nervous system can learn new patterns. It's wired for connection. For safety. For rest. And with enough small, consistent moments of safety, it can start to trust again.

Even if it takes a thousand nights.
